title: Realistic quantum device data synthesized by consumer AI and how to identify it
authors: S. M. Frolov, O. V. Kravchenko
abstract: With the advance of generative artificial intelligence (AI) synthetic texts and images have become commonplace. These capabilities offer clear benefits, but have also raised a number of ethical concerns that often have to do with misrepresenting AI outputs as genuine material. A lesser known capability of generative AI is to perform the basic analysis, processing and even synthesis of numerical data. This raises the question of whether AI can be used to imitate experimental data that an expert would consider scientifically meaningful and on par with data in the figures of peer-reviewed manuscripts? In this paper, we focus on synthesizing data inspired by well-known experiments done frequently on quantum electronic devices. This field is related to information technologies such as spintronics and quantum computing, and is considered data-rich and data-driven. We demonstrate that it is possible to generate dramatic signals associated with iconic effects such as quantum bit control, Majorana fermions, Josephson effects, quantum dots and wires using widely available ChatGPT. We find that because some of the clearest data from quantum devices can be expressed in terms of relatively basic mathematical models, AI does not need to learn on the specialized body of data. Instead, knowledge of the physics equations and of the basic features of experimental signals can go a long way towards building a realistic dataset. We also demonstrate that real data can be augmented by AI, and that AI can mimic the noise of common scientific instruments. To help assure that published data come from experiments and are not synthesized by AI, we recommend sharing large volumes of the primary data. While it is straightforward for AI to mimic a few sets of data, consistently generating long measured sequences poses sufficient barriers to the proliferation of undisclosed synthetic data.
summary: This paper investigates the ability of consumer generative AI to synthesize highly convincing data for quantum electronic devices. By simulating measurements like qubit spectroscopy and Majorana signatures, the authors show that plausible fake datasets can be generated using basic physics knowledge. The key takeaway is a call for mandatory sharing of large volumes of raw primary data to authenticate real scientific findings.
main_problem: The increasing capability of generative AI to synthesize highly realistic numerical data for quantum physics, raising concerns about misrepresenting synthetic outputs as genuine experimental results.
main_result: AI can generate convincing datasets mimicking iconic quantum effects (e.g., Majorana modes, Josephson junctions) using basic physical models, but the defense against this is sharing large volumes of raw primary data.
method: The authors demonstrate AI synthesis by prompting LLMs to write and execute Python code that simulates known physics equations and adds realistic noise/artifacts to generate synthetic measurements.
model_or_system: The focus is on quantum electronic devices, including superconducting transmon qubits, Majorana nanowires, and Quantum Point Contacts (QPCs). The underlying physics relies on established models like the qubit Hamiltonian or tunneling conductance equations.
key_observables: Spectroscopy measurements ($S_{21}$ vs. gate voltage/power), Rabi oscillations, zero-bias anomalies in tunneling spectroscopy, and quantized conductance plateaus.
important_parameters: Gate voltage, microwave power, temperature, magnetic field (implied for Majorana studies).
main_assumptions: The primary defense against AI fabrication is the mandatory sharing of large volumes of raw, unprocessed primary data files and metadata.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate synthetic transmon qubit characterization (single/two-tone spectroscopy, Rabi oscillations) and augmented Majorana nanowire tunneling spectra to suggest topological superconductivity.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the problem (AI synthesis capability), demonstrates its power across multiple quantum systems using specific examples (transmons, QPCs), and concludes by recommending data sharing as the necessary authentication standard.
why_interesting: It highlights a critical methodological challenge in modern experimental physics: distinguishing genuine discovery from sophisticated AI fabrication, which impacts how theoretical predictions are validated against empirical evidence.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Synthetic transmon data. a) Gate voltage dependence of single tone response of a superconductor-semiconductor transmon coupling to a λ/4 coplanar waveguide resonator with frequency 8.182 GHz, coupling quality factor 2000. b) Two-tone spectroscopy as a function of power showing simulated f01 =6.578 GHz and f02/2 resonances using qubit anharmonicity -380 MHz. c) Rabi oscillations “chevron” pattern using relaxation time T1 = 5.6 μS. d) Rabi oscillations as a function of power. In Figure 1 we demonstrate a set of synthetic data for a superconducting transmon qubit (31), an electronic device that is the basis for several prominent quantum computing projects (32, 33). Improvement of...

Figure 2: AI-augmented Majorana nanowire data. a) Real experimental tunneling spectroscopy data from Ref. (37). b) Panel a) augmented with a zero-bias peak. c) Panel a) augmented with higher finite-bias conductance. d) Augmentations from panels b) and c) combined. In Figure 2, we demonstrate how experimental data can be augmented using AI. For this example we start with real tunneling spectroscopy data from Ref. (37) on the so-called Majorana nanowires. In these devices, superconductor nanowires are coated with a layer of a superconductor. This induces quantized states in the nanowire which can be detected by resonant tunneling across a potential barrier. The features manifest as peaks on a...

Figure 3: Real vs. synthetic idle noise of a lock-in amplifier. Measured, fast fourier transformed and synthetic data for x-component of voltage Vx from a Stanford Research Systems SR830 lock-in amplifier using the following settings: a) integration time constant 300 ms, filter 24 dB/octave b) 100 ms, 18 dB/octave c) 100 ms, 6 dB/octave. Data plotted in virtual time (1s between data points).
